title: Kutna Hora The City of Silver

walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Main Boards
walkthrough.text: Let's get Kutna Hora set up. Place the Kutna Hora city board in the center of the table. Then place the Town Hall board to one side where everyone can reach it. Finally, place the Prospecting board nearby. These three boards form the core play area where you will build the city, acquire rights, and expand mines.

walkthrough.title: Set the Rounds Track and Player Count
walkthrough.text: Place the Rounds track where everyone can see it. If you are playing with four players, the game lasts five rounds. If you are playing with two or three players, it lasts six rounds. Place the round marker on the first space. Set the tax counter to zero on the Town Hall board. The tax rate will change throughout the game based on player actions.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Card Stands and Commodity Market
walkthrough.text: Set up the six card stands along the edge of the play area. These represent the dynamic market for the six commodities in the game: ore, wood, food, cloth, leather, and beer. Place the ore deck and the population deck on their designated card stands. The position of cards on the stands determines current prices. As cards are drawn, prices shift automatically. This is the beating heart of the economy.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Building Tiles
walkthrough.text: Separate the guild building tiles into six stacks sorted by color. Each color corresponds to one of the six guilds. Stack them in ascending order with the lowest-numbered tile on top. Then sort the public building tiles into three color stacks. Place all building stacks near the Town Hall board where players will acquire them. These buildings are what you will construct in the city.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Mining Grid
walkthrough.text: Set up the Prospecting board with the mining grid. Draw a mine setup card to determine the initial placement of rock tiles on the grid. Place rock tiles according to the card's diagram. Set aside the remaining mine tiles in a face-down supply. Throughout the game, players will carve into this grid to establish silver mines and gain ore production.

walkthrough.title: Prepare St. Barbara's Cathedral
walkthrough.text: Place the St. Barbara's Cathedral tiles near the board. These tiles represent sections of the famous cathedral that players can contribute to during the game. Contributing to the cathedral earns prestige points and is one of the key paths to scoring at game end. Sort them so the available tiles are clearly visible to all players.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Boards and Action Cards
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a player board, which tracks your production levels for each commodity. Set all production counters to their starting positions. Then each player receives their set of six action cards. These cards determine what you can do each turn. You will play five of your six cards each round, using them across three turns: two cards on your first turn, two on your second, and one on your final turn.

walkthrough.title: Assign Guilds and Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Each player receives their guild assignment showing which three of the six guilds they belong to. Your guilds determine which guild buildings you are allowed to construct. Take your starting groschen from the supply. Give each player their starting resources as indicated on their player board. The first player is determined randomly. You are now ready to begin building the city of Kutna Hora.

walkthrough.title: Game Structure and Rounds
walkthrough.text: Kutna Hora is played over five rounds with four players or six rounds with two or three players. Each round, every player takes exactly three turns. On your first and second turns you play two action cards from your hand. On your third and final turn you play one action card. This means you use five of your six action cards each round. The sixth unused card rotates each round, giving you different combinations to work with.

walkthrough.title: The Six Action Cards
walkthrough.text: Each player has the same six action cards. The actions are: Mine, Rights, Plot, Building, King's Audience, and Cathedral. When you play a card, you perform its action. On turns where you play two cards, you choose the order. The combination of which five cards you play and which one you skip each round is a critical strategic decision. Every action card has a specific purpose that feeds into the larger economic engine.

walkthrough.title: Action: Mine
walkthrough.text: When you play the Mine action card, you draw a set of mine tiles from the supply and choose one to place on the mining grid on the Prospecting board. You must pay the wood cost printed on the tile. Once placed, the mine tile increases your ore production on your player board. The mining grid has rows, and at the end of the game the player with the most tiles in a row earns bonus points for that row. Mines are your primary source of ore, which fuels the economy.

walkthrough.title: Action: Rights
walkthrough.text: The Rights action lets you acquire a building tile from the Town Hall board. You pay a permit cost in groschen to claim the tile. Guild building tiles can only be acquired if you belong to the matching guild. Public building tiles can be acquired by anyone. Taking a building tile is only the first step. You still need to claim a plot and then construct the building in a separate action. Planning your acquisition around your guild memberships and available plots is essential.

walkthrough.title: Action: Plot
walkthrough.text: The Plot action lets you claim a plot of land in the city of Kutna Hora on the main board. You pay groschen for the plot. The city is laid out in a grid, and where you place your plot marker matters enormously. Buildings on your plots will interact with adjacent buildings, and the city layout affects scoring at the end of the game. Plots are limited, so claiming good locations early gives you a major advantage.

walkthrough.title: Action: Building
walkthrough.text: The Building action lets you construct a building tile you previously acquired on a plot you previously claimed. You pay the building's wood cost. When a guild building is constructed, it increases your production of the associated commodity and shifts the market indicators on the card stands. This is how the dynamic economy works. Building a cloth guild building increases your cloth production and changes cloth's market price for everyone. Public buildings have unique effects but do not belong to any player.

walkthrough.title: Action: King's Audience
walkthrough.text: The King's Audience action gives you a special opportunity that changes each round. It can provide bonus resources, let you manipulate the market, contribute to civic projects, or gain reputation. The exact benefits rotate and are revealed at the start of each round, so you need to evaluate whether the current offering is worth spending one of your precious action card slots. Sometimes the audience is game-changing; other times you skip it for a more reliable action.

walkthrough.title: Action: Cathedral
walkthrough.text: The Cathedral action lets you contribute to the construction of St. Barbara's Cathedral. You select an available cathedral tile and pay its cost. Contributing earns you prestige points immediately and positions you for significant end-game scoring. Cathedral contributions are competitive because tiles are limited. The cathedral is one of the most reliable scoring paths in the game, but it requires consistent investment across multiple rounds to maximize.

walkthrough.title: The Dynamic Economy
walkthrough.text: The economy of Kutna Hora is the game's signature mechanism. Six commodities have prices that fluctuate based on player actions. When you build a guild building, it shifts the market indicator for that commodity. When ore or population cards are drawn from the card stands, prices change. Supply and demand are simulated through these shifts. A commodity that many players produce will drop in price, reducing everyone's income from it. Understanding and manipulating these price movements is what separates good players from great ones.

walkthrough.title: Production and Income
walkthrough.text: Your player board tracks your production level for each commodity. At specific points in the game, you earn income based on your production levels multiplied by the current market prices. If you produce a lot of a commodity whose price has crashed, you earn very little. If you produce even a moderate amount of a high-priced commodity, you earn handsomely. This interplay between production volume and market price is the core economic tension. You want to produce what is valuable, not just what is plentiful.

walkthrough.title: Taxes and Reputation
walkthrough.text: The tax counter on the Town Hall board tracks the current tax rate. Certain actions and events cause the tax rate to rise or fall. At income time, taxes are subtracted from your earnings. Building public buildings and contributing to the cathedral can improve your reputation, while ignoring civic responsibilities can hurt it. Your reputation affects your standing during patrician scoring and may provide bonuses or penalties at various checkpoints in the game.

walkthrough.title: Patrician Scoring
walkthrough.text: At the end of the last three rounds of the game, patrician scoring occurs. This is an intermediate scoring phase where players earn points based on their current standing in several categories. Your reputation, building presence in the city, and economic contributions are all evaluated. Patrician scoring rewards players who are building a balanced and productive position rather than saving everything for a single end-game push.

walkthrough.title: End-Game Scoring
walkthrough.text: After the final round, end-game scoring begins. You score points for matching dots on adjacent buildings in the city, which rewards thoughtful plot placement and building adjacency. You also score for mine row dominance on the Prospecting board, where the player with the most mine tiles in each row earns bonus points. Cathedral contributions, remaining groschen, and various building bonuses are also tallied. The player with the most total points wins.

walkthrough.title: Population and the Ore Deck
walkthrough.text: The population deck and the ore deck sit on card stands and serve dual purposes. Drawing from these decks provides resources or workers, but each draw also shifts the position of cards on the stands, changing commodity prices. A heavily drawn ore deck can crash ore prices, while an untouched population deck might keep food and cloth prices high. Every draw has a ripple effect on the economy, making timing and awareness of deck depletion critical to your strategy.

walkthrough.title: Your First Round Begins
walkthrough.text: Welcome to 14th century Kutna Hora. You have six action cards in hand and will play five of them this round across three turns. Look at your guild assignments first. You belong to three guilds, and these determine which buildings you can construct. Check which commodities your guilds are associated with. These will be your primary production paths. For your first turn, you will play two action cards. Let's start by establishing your economic foundation.

walkthrough.title: Establish Your Mine
walkthrough.text: Play the Mine action card as your first action. Draw the available mine tiles and examine the mining grid. Look for a spot that gives you good ore production and positions you well for row dominance at the end of the game. Pay the wood cost and place your tile. Your ore production counter increases on your player board. Ore is the fundamental commodity of Kutna Hora, and early mining establishes your income floor. Notice how the ore price on the card stand may shift as a result.

walkthrough.title: Claim Your Rights
walkthrough.text: For your second action on this first turn, play the Rights card. Look at the Town Hall board and identify a guild building that matches one of your three guilds. Pay the permit cost and take the tile. You now hold building rights but have not built anything yet. Think about which commodity this building will boost your production of, and check its current market price on the card stands. If that commodity's price is high, this building will be very profitable once constructed.

walkthrough.title: Secure a Plot and Build
walkthrough.text: On your second turn, play Plot and Building together. First, use Plot to claim a location on the city board. Look at what has already been placed and think about adjacency. The dots on building tiles need to match neighboring tiles for end-game scoring, so position your plot where you can create matches. Then play Building to construct the tile you acquired last turn on this new plot. Pay the wood cost. Your production of the associated commodity increases and the market shifts. Watch how the price changes. You just participated in the dynamic economy.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Market
walkthrough.text: Before your final turn, pause and read the market. Look at all six card stands. Which commodities are currently expensive? Which are cheap? If multiple players are building in the same guild, that commodity's price is probably dropping from oversupply. If a commodity has high prices and you have a guild building that produces it, that is where your money will come from. Now decide your fifth and final action card for this round. If the King's Audience offers something useful, take it. Otherwise, consider Cathedral for long-term scoring or another Mine for more ore production.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Income Phase
walkthrough.text: After all players complete their three turns, the income phase occurs. Your income is calculated by multiplying your production levels by current market prices for each commodity, then subtracting taxes. Notice how income is driven by two factors you partially control: your production levels and the market prices. You can boost production by building, but prices shift based on everyone's collective building decisions. A player who builds the same guild building as you is diluting both of your incomes. This is why diversification and market awareness matter.

walkthrough.title: Planning for Patrician Scoring
walkthrough.text: As the game progresses into the later rounds, patrician scoring begins at the end of the last three rounds. Check what the patrician scoring evaluates. Your reputation, building presence in the city, and your economic contributions all factor in. If you have been ignoring reputation by skipping public buildings and cathedral contributions, you may lose points here. Balance your economic engine building with civic investments. The players who score well in patrician rounds often win the game because those points compound over three scoring opportunities.

walkthrough.title: Adjacency and Dot Matching
walkthrough.text: Let's practice a key end-game concept. Look at the buildings already in the city. Each building tile has colored dots on its edges. When adjacent buildings share matching dots, they score bonus points at the end of the game. When you claim plots and build, try to create chains of matching dots. This means you need to plan your Rights acquisition and Plot placement together. Do not just grab the cheapest plot. Grab the plot that creates the most dot matches with existing or planned buildings.

walkthrough.title: The Mining Grid Endgame
walkthrough.text: Return your attention to the Prospecting board. Each row on the mining grid awards end-game bonus points to the player with the most mine tiles in that row. If you have been mining consistently, you may dominate one or two rows. But be careful. Another player might place one strategic tile to tie or overtake you in a key row. In later rounds, consider whether a Mine action to secure row dominance is worth more than another building or cathedral contribution. The mining bonuses can be substantial.

walkthrough.title: Cathedral Investment Pays Off
walkthrough.text: If you have been contributing to St. Barbara's Cathedral throughout the game, your end-game scoring will reflect it. Cathedral tiles earn prestige points that can be the difference between winning and losing. Players who invest early and consistently in the cathedral build a reliable scoring foundation that does not depend on volatile market prices. Think of the cathedral as your insurance policy. Even if the economy turns against your commodity focus, cathedral points hold their value.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations — Key Takeaways
walkthrough.text: You have now walked through a full practice game of Kutna Hora. The essential lessons are: establish mines early for a steady ore income floor, build guild buildings in commodities with strong prices, watch the market before committing to a production strategy, invest in reputation through public buildings and cathedral to score well in patrician rounds, plan your plot placement for dot matching, and compete for mining row dominance. The dynamic economy is what makes every game different. No two games will have the same price trajectories, so stay flexible and react to what other players are doing.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text: You are ready to play Kutna Hora: The City of Silver. Check out the General Tips section for strategic fundamentals that will help you make better decisions from your very first game. Use the Appendix as a quick reference for any terms or concepts you want to look up during play. And whenever you have a question, tap the Q&A tab to ask your personal GM Guide for an instant answer. Good luck building your silver empire.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the traps that catch new players. Overproducing a single commodity without watching the market leads to crashing your own income. Ignoring reputation and civic contributions means losing big during patrician scoring rounds. Claiming plots without thinking about dot adjacency wastes end-game points. Skipping mines entirely leaves you without the ore income that funds everything else. Finally, using all six actions every round without skipping strategically means you never have a focused, high-impact turn.

walkthrough.title: Diversify Your Production
walkthrough.text: Your three guild memberships give you access to three types of guild buildings. Use at least two of them. If you pour everything into a single commodity, every building you place pushes that commodity's price further down. Diversifying across two or three commodities protects you from price crashes in any one market. Think of it like an investment portfolio. When cloth crashes because three players are building cloth buildings, your leather and beer production keep generating strong income because fewer players are competing in those markets.

walkthrough.title: Watch What Others Build
walkthrough.text: The dynamic economy means your income depends on what everyone does, not just your own actions. Pay close attention to which guilds other players belong to and what they are building. If two opponents share your strongest guild, that commodity will become oversaturated. Pivot to your other guilds before the price collapses. Conversely, if no one else is producing a commodity you have access to, lean into it aggressively. Scarcity keeps prices high, and monopoly production of a scarce commodity is extremely profitable.

walkthrough.title: Balance Short-Term and Long-Term Scoring
walkthrough.text: Kutna Hora rewards players who score from multiple sources. Income from production is your short-term scoring engine. Patrician scoring at the end of the last three rounds is your mid-term payoff. Dot matching, mine row dominance, and cathedral contributions are your long-term end-game scores. The best players feed all three timelines simultaneously. Do not sacrifice your income engine for end-game scoring or ignore end-game scoring to maximize income. Find the balance that keeps you competitive at every scoring checkpoint.

walkthrough.title: Action Card Selection Matters
walkthrough.text: Choosing which action card to skip each round is one of the most important decisions in the game. In early rounds, you might skip Cathedral to focus on building your economic engine. In later rounds, you might skip Mine because you have enough ore production. In the final round, skipping King's Audience to guarantee your last building and a cathedral contribution might clinch the win. There is no universally correct card to skip. Evaluate each round based on your current position, the market, and what scoring opportunities remain.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Building Actions
walkthrough.text: The sequence of Rights, Plot, Building requires careful timing across multiple turns. You cannot build without first acquiring rights and a plot. Plan two to three turns ahead so that when your Building turn arrives, you have both the tile and the plot ready. Nothing is worse than holding a building tile for an entire round because you forgot to claim a plot. Experienced players pipeline their actions so they always have a building ready to construct each round, maximizing their production growth.

walkthrough.title: Tax Awareness Saves You Money
walkthrough.text: The tax rate can creep up on you if you are not paying attention. Every time taxes increase, it cuts into everyone's income. If you see the tax counter climbing, factor that into your production calculations. Sometimes it is worth investing in actions that lower taxes or improve your reputation to reduce your tax burden. A small investment in civic responsibility can save you more groschen over the remaining rounds than an additional building would generate. Watch the tax counter every round.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Overproducing 1 commodity without watching the market = income crash
Ignoring reputation = losing big in patrician scoring
Claiming plots without dot adjacency planning = wasted end-game points
Skipping mines entirely = no income floor to fund your plans

walkthrough.title: The Market Manipulation Strategy
walkthrough.text: Advanced players do not just react to the market. They actively manipulate it. If you know an opponent is heavily invested in cloth production, you can take actions that increase cloth supply and crash the price right before an income phase. Build a cloth guild building yourself not for the production, but to depress the price and cripple their income. Conversely, you can time your builds to avoid oversaturating your own commodities. Build your last guild building right after an income phase so you benefit from the current price before the market shifts downward. Market timing is everything at high levels of play.

walkthrough.title: The Guild Specialization Approach
walkthrough.text: While diversification is safe, a focused guild specialization can be devastatingly effective if the table conditions are right. If you are the only player in a particular guild, you have a monopoly on that commodity's production buildings. Every building you place increases your production without adding competition. The price stays high because supply grows slowly from a single player. Identify your unique guild early. If no one else shares it, go all in. If someone else joins, pivot immediately to your second-most unique guild. The monopoly advantage compounds over every income phase.

walkthrough.title: Mining Grid Control
walkthrough.text: The mining grid is an often-overlooked battleground. Players who treat mining as an afterthought miss substantial end-game points. Advanced players identify which rows will be contested and which can be locked down cheaply. Placing mine tiles early in rows where opponents have little presence creates a dominant position that is expensive for them to challenge. Sometimes placing a single mine tile in an opponent's strongest row is worth more than the ore production it generates, because it prevents them from scoring those row dominance points unchallenged. Think of the mining grid as a territorial control game within the larger economic game.

walkthrough.title: Cathedral Timing and Denial
walkthrough.text: Cathedral contributions offer some of the most reliable points in the game, but they are also a denial opportunity. Each cathedral tile taken is one fewer available to opponents. If you notice an opponent building a cathedral-focused strategy, accelerating your own cathedral contributions reduces their available options. The reverse is also true. If no one else is investing in the cathedral, the tiles remain available for you to claim at your leisure. Read the table's cathedral interest level and adjust your timing accordingly. Going early denies opponents; going late means less competition for your other actions.

walkthrough.title: Dot Matching Optimization
walkthrough.text: End-game dot matching is the most geometrically challenging scoring category. Advanced players draft building tiles partly based on which dots appear on the tile edges, not just the building's economic benefit. When you use the Rights action, check which tiles have dots that match what is already in the city or what you plan to place. When you use the Plot action, choose locations that maximize adjacency with multiple existing buildings. The best players create clusters of three or four mutually matching buildings that score points in every direction. A single well-placed building with four matching neighbors scores more than two buildings with one match each.

walkthrough.title: Reputation as a Weapon
walkthrough.text: Reputation affects patrician scoring, which happens three times in the late game. A high reputation does not just help you. A low reputation actively hurts opponents who neglected civic investment. Advanced players build public buildings and contribute to the cathedral not just for the direct points, but to establish a reputation lead that scores consistently across multiple patrician rounds. If you can build a reputation advantage by the third-to-last round, it pays dividends three times. Meanwhile, opponents who focused purely on economic output find themselves losing ground at every patrician evaluation.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Action Card Optimization
walkthrough.text: In the final round, every action card choice is magnified. There is no future round to recover from a mistake. Advanced players plan their final round from two rounds out, ensuring they have the rights, plots, and resources needed to execute a perfect closing sequence. Your last Building should create a high-value dot match. Your last Mine should secure a contested row. Your last Cathedral contribution should lock in prestige before opponents can respond. The player who enters the final round with all their pieces in position has already won half the battle. Do not improvise your endgame. Plan it.

summary.title: The Market Manipulation Strategy
bullets: Build in opponent's guild to crash their commodity price before income
Time your own builds right after income phases to protect your prices
Market timing separates good from great players
Every build shifts prices — use that offensively, not just reactively

summary.title: The Guild Specialization Approach
bullets: Monopoly on a guild = uncontested high-price production
If you're the only player in a guild, go all in
If someone joins your guild, pivot immediately to your next-best
Monopoly advantage compounds over every income phase

summary.title: Mining Grid Control
bullets: Identify contestable vs. lockable rows early
Place 1 mine in opponent's strongest row to block dominance
Think of the mining grid as territorial control
Mining row points are substantial and often undervalued

summary.title: Cathedral Timing and Denial
bullets: Each tile you take is one fewer for opponents
Accelerate contributions if opponent is cathedral-focused
If no one else invests, take tiles at leisure — less competition elsewhere
Read the table's cathedral interest and adjust timing

summary.title: Dot Matching Optimization
bullets: Draft building tiles partly for edge dots, not just economics
Choose plots to maximize multi-building adjacency
Create clusters of 3-4 mutually matching buildings
1 building with 4 matching neighbors > 2 buildings with 1 match each

summary.title: Reputation as a Weapon
bullets: High reputation scores across 3 patrician rounds = compounding advantage
Low reputation punishes opponents who neglected civic investment
Build reputation lead by 3rd-to-last round for max payoff
Cathedral + public buildings = reliable reputation boosters

summary.title: Endgame Action Card Optimization
bullets: Plan final round from 2 rounds out
Last Building = high-value dot match
Last Mine = secure contested row; last Cathedral = lock in prestige
Don't improvise your endgame — plan it

entries.definition: The currency of the game. Used to pay for plots, building permits, mine tiles, cathedral contributions, and various other costs. Earned primarily through income phases where production is multiplied by market prices. Remaining groschen score points at end of game.

A fundamental commodity produced by mines on the Prospecting board. Ore production is tracked on your player board. Income from ore depends on its current market price. Heavy mining by multiple players can depress ore prices.

A commodity required as a building cost for mine tiles and buildings. Wood costs are printed on the tiles. Wood also has a market price that affects production income. Managing your wood expenses is key to efficient building.

A commodity whose market price is influenced by population deck draws and building activity. Food production comes from specific guild buildings. Its price tends to remain high if the population deck is not heavily drawn.

A commodity produced by the cloth guild. Building cloth guild buildings increases your cloth production and shifts the cloth market indicator. Like all commodities, oversupply from multiple players building cloth will depress its price.

A commodity produced by the leather guild. Leather production buildings work like other guild buildings, increasing your production counter while shifting the market indicator. A less-contested leather market can be very profitable.

A commodity produced by the beer guild. Beer production follows the same dynamic market rules as other commodities. Its price fluctuates based on building activity and deck draws. Beer guild buildings are available only to players who belong to the beer guild.

The core mechanism of Kutna Hora. Six commodities have prices that change based on player actions. Building guild buildings shifts market indicators. Drawing from ore and population decks moves card positions on stands. Income depends on production multiplied by these fluctuating prices.

Physical stands that hold the ore and population decks. The position of cards on the stands determines current commodity prices. As cards are drawn, remaining cards shift position, changing prices. The card stands are the visual representation of the dynamic market.

The current value of a commodity, determined by the position of market indicators on the card stands. Prices change when guild buildings are built, when cards are drawn from decks, and through certain special actions. Higher prices mean more income per unit of production.

A marker on your player board that tracks how much of each commodity you produce. Production increases when you build guild buildings or place mine tiles. During income phases, production level is multiplied by the current market price to determine your earnings.

A phase at the end of each round where players earn groschen. Income equals the sum of all your production levels multiplied by their respective market prices, minus the current tax rate. This is your primary source of groschen throughout the game.

A tracker on the Town Hall board that shows the current tax rate. Taxes are subtracted from income during each income phase. Certain actions and events raise or lower the tax rate. Starts at zero at the beginning of the game.

A measure of your civic standing. Improved by building public buildings and contributing to St. Barbara's Cathedral. Affects scoring during patrician scoring rounds. High reputation can provide bonuses while low reputation may result in penalties.

One of six organizations in Kutna Hora, each associated with a commodity. Each player belongs to three guilds, which determine which guild building tiles they can acquire and construct. Guild membership is assigned at the start of the game and does not change.

One of six action cards. Draw mine tiles from the supply, choose one, and place it on the mining grid on the Prospecting board. Pay the wood cost. Increases your ore production. Positions you for mine row dominance scoring at end of game.

One of six action cards. Acquire a building tile from the Town Hall board by paying a permit cost in groschen. Guild buildings require matching guild membership. Public buildings are available to all players. The tile is held until you can build it with the Building action.

One of six action cards. Claim a plot of land on the Kutna Hora city board by paying groschen. Location matters for building adjacency and dot matching at end of game. Plots are limited, so strategic placement is critical.

One of six action cards. Construct a previously acquired building tile on a previously claimed plot. Pay the wood cost. Guild buildings increase your commodity production and shift market indicators. Public buildings have unique effects but no owner.

One of six action cards. Provides a special benefit that changes each round. May offer bonus resources, market manipulation, reputation boosts, or civic contributions. The current offering is revealed at the start of each round. Strategic value varies from round to round.

One of six action cards. Contribute a tile to St. Barbara's Cathedral by paying its cost. Earns immediate prestige points and contributes to end-game scoring. Cathedral tiles are limited and competitive. Consistent investment maximizes returns.

A building tile associated with a specific guild and commodity. Can only be acquired by players who belong to that guild. When constructed, increases the owner's production of the associated commodity and shifts the market indicator, changing prices for all players.

A building tile that any player can acquire and construct regardless of guild membership. Public buildings do not belong to any player once built and do not increase commodity production. They have unique effects and often improve the builder's reputation.

A landmark under construction during the game. Players contribute cathedral tiles by using the Cathedral action and paying costs. Contributions earn prestige points and count toward end-game scoring. One of the most reliable scoring paths in the game.

A grid on the Prospecting board where players place mine tiles using the Mine action. Organized into rows. At end of game, the player with the most mine tiles in each row earns bonus points for row dominance. Strategic tile placement creates territorial control.
A board containing the mining grid where mine tiles are placed. Also tracks mine-related information and row dominance scoring. Central to the ore production and mining strategy aspects of the game.

A board where building tiles are displayed for acquisition using the Rights action. Guild buildings are arranged in six color-coded stacks. Public buildings are in three stacks. Also displays the tax counter that tracks the current tax rate.

A personal board that tracks your production levels for each commodity using production counters. Shows your current output for ore, wood, food, cloth, leather, and beer. Production levels are multiplied by market prices during income phases.

The main game board representing the city. Contains a grid of plots where players claim land and construct buildings. Building placement and adjacency determine dot matching scoring at end of game. The physical layout of the growing city.

A track showing the progression of rounds. Four-player games use five rounds; two- and three-player games use six rounds. The round marker advances after each round completes. Patrician scoring occurs at the end of the last three rounds.

A card drawn during setup that determines the initial placement of rock tiles on the mining grid. Different setup cards create different starting configurations, ensuring the mining landscape varies from game to game.

A set of six cards each player holds: Mine, Rights, Plot, Building, King's Audience, and Cathedral. Each round you play five of six across three turns (2 + 2 + 1). The sixth card is skipped. Which card to skip is a key strategic decision each round.

An intermediate scoring phase that occurs at the end of the last three rounds. Evaluates players on reputation, building presence in the city, and economic contributions. Rewards balanced play and civic investment. Points from patrician scoring compound across three rounds.

An end-game scoring category. Building tiles have colored dots on their edges. When adjacent buildings share matching dots, players score bonus points. Requires coordinated Rights acquisition, Plot placement, and Building construction for maximum effect.

An end-game scoring category. For each row on the mining grid, the player with the most mine tiles earns bonus points. Ties may be broken by specific rules. Strategic mine placement to control rows can yield substantial end-game points.

The groschen cost to acquire a building tile from the Town Hall board using the Rights action. Different tiles have different permit costs. Must be paid immediately when the tile is claimed. Higher-level buildings typically cost more.

When multiple players build guild buildings for the same commodity, increasing total supply and depressing the market price. Overproduction reduces everyone's income from that commodity. The primary economic risk of following the same strategy as opponents.
